meta.title: CSR vs SSR vs RSC for a React product experience
meta.summary: A React commerce page needs SEO, fast first render, large product data, faceted filters, and client-side interactions. Would you defend CSR, SSR, or RSC?

scenario: You are designing a React commerce experience in a framework that supports client-side rendering, server-side rendering, and React Server Components. The page is SEO-sensitive, should load category content and product data quickly, includes faceted filters and add-to-cart interactions, and may show some user-specific recommendations after login. The team also cares about keeping client JavaScript and hydration cost under control. In the interview, the panel asks which primary rendering direction you would defend: CSR-first, SSR-first, or RSC-first with client components only where interactivity is needed.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other options still become the better answer?

options.label: CSR-first
options.summary: Keeps most UI logic on the client and can work well when the experience behaves more like an app than a crawlable content surface.
whenItWins: SEO and first HTML content are not major concerns, such as in an authenticated dashboard or internal tool.
The page is highly personalized and most meaningful data only appears after the user is already interacting on the client.
The team values one client-heavy execution model and the product can tolerate more JavaScript and delayed data after boot.
watchOutFor: For SEO-sensitive pages with large initial content, CSR pushes more work onto the client and often delays meaningful content behind download, parse, and hydration.
A weak answer picks CSR because it feels simpler without discussing crawler behavior, first render, or client bundle cost.

options.label: SSR-first
options.summary: Delivers HTML from the server for a stronger first render and SEO story while still hydrating the page into a client React app afterward.
whenItWins: The page is crawlable, the first render matters, and the team needs a mature server-rendering model without adopting RSC-specific boundaries yet.
The initial content should appear quickly in HTML, but much of the page will still become a normal client component tree after hydration.
Framework or team constraints make RSC adoption risky right now, yet pure CSR would be too expensive for SEO and initial performance.
watchOutFor: SSR still hydrates the rendered client tree, so it does not automatically solve client JavaScript cost for heavy pages.
A weak answer treats SSR as the universal safe middle without asking whether the page has large server-owned regions that should never become client code.

options.label: RSC-first with client islands
options.summary: Keeps server-owned UI and data fetching on the server, then introduces client components only for the interactive parts that truly need them.
whenItWins: Large parts of the page are mostly data-driven or content-driven and do not need to ship as interactive client code.
You want a better boundary between server-owned data work and client-only interactive islands like filters, carts, or controls.
The product cares about SEO, first render, and reducing client bundle and hydration cost without giving up targeted interactivity.
watchOutFor: RSC adds architectural complexity, framework coupling, and new mental models around boundaries, caching, and client/server splits.
A weak answer says RSC is the future and should replace everything, without explaining what still must remain client-side or how team maturity affects the choice.

decisionMatrix.title: Highly interactive app, low SEO pressure
decisionMatrix.prompt: Most value appears after login, content is user-specific, and the page behaves more like a client application than a crawlable public surface.

cells.note: This is easier to defend when client interactivity dominates and the product is not paying for slower public first render.
Still possible, but the server-rendered shell may not buy much if the meaningful content appears only after client-side app logic.
Viable if there is still meaningful server-owned data, but not automatically the most valuable complexity for this shape of product.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: SEO matters, first content matters, product data is large, and only parts of the page truly need client interactivity.

cells.note: Harder to defend because it pushes too much initial work and JavaScript onto the client for a page that should show real content early.
A solid fallback if the team wants a mature server-rendered path, but it still hydrates more client code than the page may actually need.
This is easiest to defend when most of the page is server-owned and only selected islands need client behavior.

decisionMatrix.title: Strong platform constraints or immature RSC adoption
decisionMatrix.prompt: The team is not ready for RSC boundaries yet, or deployment and caching complexity make the newer model risky in the short term.

cells.note: Still hard to justify if public SEO and first render remain important.
This becomes the pragmatic answer when RSC would introduce more organizational risk than value right now.
Technically attractive, but weaker if the team cannot operate the boundary model confidently yet.

evaluationDimensions.title: How important are SEO and first HTML content?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is often the first split. Public category and product pages usually care more about crawlability and early content than authenticated app surfaces do.

evaluationDimensions.title: How expensive is client JavaScript and hydration?
evaluationDimensions.description: SSR improves first HTML delivery, but it can still hydrate a large client tree. RSC becomes more compelling when that client cost is the real bottleneck.

evaluationDimensions.title: Which parts of the UI really need to be client components?
evaluationDimensions.description: If large parts of the page are mainly data and markup, shipping them as client code may be unnecessary work.

evaluationDimensions.title: Where do caching and personalization boundaries sit?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should separate globally cacheable content from user-specific islands and explain how that affects rendering strategy.

evaluationDimensions.title: Can the team operate the architecture well?
evaluationDimensions.description: The technically strongest model is not always the operationally strongest model if the team cannot reason about server/client boundaries, caching, and debugging yet.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean RSC-first, but only with explicit client boundaries
strongAnswer.summary: This page has strong SEO and first-render requirements, plus large server-owned data, but only parts of it truly need client interactivity. That makes an RSC-first approach the most defensible for this exact prompt.
reasoning: Pure CSR is hard to justify because the page should show meaningful content early and should not force the client to do all the initial data and rendering work.
SSR is a respectable fallback and still much easier to defend than CSR here, but it often hydrates more of the page than necessary if large regions are mostly server-owned content and data.

RSC gives a stronger boundary for this prompt: keep product content, data fetching, and server-owned layout on the server, then introduce client components only for faceted filters, cart controls, and other interactive islands.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would defend an RSC-first page with explicit client islands for the interactive parts. If team or platform constraints made that risky right now, I would fall back to SSR before I would fall back to CSR.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not just use SSR and keep the architecture simpler?
interviewerPushback.answer: That is a very fair fallback, and I would absolutely choose it over CSR here if the team is not ready for RSC. I would still lean RSC for this prompt because large parts of the page look server-owned, so reducing client bundle and hydration cost is part of the value, not just getting initial HTML out quickly.

interviewerPushback.question: Is RSC replacing SSR?
interviewerPushback.answer: Not really. I would explain it as a boundary model rather than a total replacement story. You still care about server delivery and streaming, but RSC changes which parts of the tree stay server-owned and which parts become client components.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the page became far more app-like, heavily personalized, and less SEO-sensitive, I would move closer to CSR. If the product shape stayed the same but the team could not operate RSC well yet, I would move toward SSR as the more pragmatic choice.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use RSC because it is the newest and best React model now.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds trend-driven, ignores team and product constraints, and teaches no boundary for when SSR or CSR still fit better.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use SSR or RSC because SEO matters and CSR would be weaker.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a sharper explanation of hydration cost, server-owned UI, and why RSC beats SSR specifically for this exact prompt.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would lean RSC-first with explicit client islands. The page is SEO-sensitive and content-heavy, so pure CSR would push too much initial work to the browser. SSR is a solid fallback, but it still hydrates more of the page than needed if large product and content regions are mainly server-owned. I would keep data fetching and server-owned layout in RSC, then use client components only for the interactive parts like faceted filters and cart controls. If the team could not operate that boundary model confidently yet, I would fall back to SSR.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it ties the rendering choice to product shape, JavaScript cost, and team maturity instead of treating the newest model as an automatic winner.

answerFramework: Start by naming the product shape: public SEO-sensitive surface, authenticated app surface, or a hybrid.
Explain where the page's server-owned and client-owned boundaries probably belong.
State which rendering direction you would choose for this prompt and why.
Compare the other options in terms of first render, hydration cost, and operational complexity.
End with a pragmatic fallback, for example: RSC if the boundary model is worth it, SSR if the team is not ready, CSR only if the product is truly client-app shaped.
antiPatterns: Treating RSC as a universal replacement for SSR and CSR without discussing boundaries or team readiness.
Saying SSR solves everything while ignoring hydration and client JavaScript cost.
Picking CSR for a public content-heavy page without addressing SEO and first render.
Talking only about framework trends instead of product shape, cacheability, and operational tradeoffs.
